title: Repairing a rotted window sill
subtitle: Cut out the soft wood, apply the epoxy consolidant and filler, re-paint
excerpt: How to repair a softwood window sill with localised wet rot: testing the extent of the damage, treating sound wood with consolidant, removing soft material, applying two-part epoxy wood filler, shaping, sanding, and painting.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Ronseal, Sadolin, and Osmo technical data sheets (open-access). Historic England guidance on repair of timber windows.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fungal decay of timber caused by prolonged exposure to moisture. The wood becomes soft, dark, and fibrous and can be pushed in with a finger. Confined to the wet area; once dried out it stops spreading.

A low-viscosity two-part epoxy resin that soaks into fibrous, degraded timber and hardens it in place, strengthening it without removal. Used before applying epoxy wood filler to soft but structurally attached wood.

content.text: in a window sill is a standard repair for a painted-joinery window. The rot occurs where water has been lying on the sill face (failed paint, lifted putty at the glass joint) or where water-trapping detail has held moisture against end grain. The two-part epoxy system (consolidant and filler) is the correct repair for damage affecting up to about 30 percent of the cross-section; for deeper structural damage, a timber splice repair or full sill replacement is the right answer.

Probe the sill surface with a screwdriver or bradawl. Soft areas that the point sinks into easily are active or recent wet rot. Harder areas that resist are sound. Define the full extent of the soft area before starting the repair.

Apply a hot-air gun to the soft area, working slowly across the surface, to drive off moisture from the wood fibres. Allow the sill to cool and dry for at least 24 hours before proceeding. Epoxy consolidant and filler will not bond to wet wood.
Step 2, remove soft and loose material.

Using the chisel and the scraper, remove all material that is soft, fibrous, or lifting. Cut back to firm wood at the edges of the repair; leave any soft material that is hard to access and will instead be consolidated with the epoxy resin.

according to the manufacturer's ratio. Brush it liberally into the exposed wood surface and allow it to soak in. Apply a second coat if the first is fully absorbed within 5 minutes. The consolidant hardens any remaining fibrous wood to provide a firm base for the filler.
Step 4, apply the epoxy wood filler.

Once the consolidant has cured (check the data sheet; typically 3 to 6 hours at 15 degrees), mix the two-part epoxy wood filler to an even, streak-free colour. Pack it firmly into the repair using the filling knife, slightly overfilling to allow for sanding back. Build up in layers if the repair is deeper than 10 mm; each layer should be cured before the next is applied.

Once the filler has cured fully (at minimum 2 hours, longer in cold weather), shape it to match the sill profile with 80-grit on the sanding block, progressing through 120 and 180. Restore any sill drip groove on the underside if it was filled: cut it back with a chisel.

Apply an oil-based primer to the repaired area. Epoxy filler has low absorption but needs primer adhesion for the topcoat. Allow to dry, apply the undercoat, then two full-gloss topcoats. Extend the new paint to the full sill surface to reseal the surrounding joinery.

attrs.symptom: Epoxy filler cracks within the first year
attrs.cause: The repair was applied too thick in one layer, or the consolidant was not allowed to cure before the filler was applied.
attrs.fix: Remove the affected filler. Re-apply consolidant, allow full cure, and build up filler in two or three thinner layers.

attrs.symptom: Rot returns in the repaired area within two years
attrs.cause: The moisture source was not identified and fixed before the repair.
attrs.fix: Check the putty joint at the glass, the sill's back-edge junction with the frame, and the paint condition on the sill face. Repair or renew the source before re-treating the sill.
